candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made non-circular and genuinely explanatory rather than redescriptive? It says mathematics and physics fit because both target invariants, but this risks merely restating the phenomenon unless it explains why reality has invariant structure, why humans were able to abstract the right kinds of invariants, and why highly purified mathematics so often lands on structures later instantiated in physics rather than mostly missing them.

What principled boundary distinguishes mathematics that is genealogically connected to physically instantiated invariants from mathematics that remains unapplied? The conjecture explains delayed applicability by mathematical overproduction plus later physical selection, but it leaves open whether there are identifiable structural markers, historical pathways, or selection mechanisms that predict applicability instead of explaining it only after the fact.

How should the conjecture handle mathematical domains whose origins and development seem weakly tied to practical invariant-tracking, yet still become central to physics? If major parts of modern mathematics arose from internal conceptual problems, aesthetic criteria, or proof-theoretic pressures rather than direct compression of repeatable worldly structure, then the proposed common ancestry may be too narrow or historically incomplete.
